title: Differences in Community Recognition of Autism Between Special Education and Clinicians: Insights from Public Health Surveillance in Arizona

summary.hook: This poster compares how autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is “recognized” across education (autism special education exceptionality) versus clinical systems (ASD ICD codes / diagnostic statements) using Arizona ADDM surveillance data on 8-year-olds from 2018/2020/2022 (n=873).
summary.summary_30s: This poster compares how autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is “recognized” across education (autism special education exceptionality) versus clinical systems (ASD ICD codes / diagnostic statements) using Arizona ADDM surveillance data on 8-year-olds from 2018/2020/2022 (n=873). The authors find limited overlap between education and clinical recognition categories (education-only 48%, clinical-only 18%, both 29%, neither 6%) and near-zero agreement between special education exceptionality and ASD ICD coding among children with multiple record sources (κ \= -0.001). Multiple record sources are strongly associated with higher likelihood of recognition, especially for having an ASD ICD code (OR=12.57, Holm p<0.001).
talktrack_2m: Focus: Assess whether record source availability is associated with differences in education and clinical recognition of ASD
Approach: Population: 8-year-old children in Arizona ADDM surveillance cycles Primary outcome: Membership in education/clinical recognition categories (as defined in results/figure)
Finding: Recognition categories show limited overlap between educational and clinical systems (n=873): 48% education-only, 18% ICD-only, 29% both, 6% neither.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Educational and clinical systems identify overlapping but distinct subsets of children with ASD. Special education exceptionality and ICD code for autism do not mutually predict each other → suggests system-level discordance. This is consistent with the idea that special education requirements (IDEA) and clinical diagnostic criteria (DSM-5) focus on different aspects of ASD. Public health surveillance for autism may need reassessment: are educational and clinical recognition “the same thing,” and should diagnostic criteria be revised to include both educational and clinical experiences? Findings need full ADDM Network confirmation. Implications (poster-grounded): Multiple record sources increase likelihood of recognition by both systems. Some characteristics decrease likelihood of recognition. Open question raised: fundamental access issue vs two distinct subpopulations. Need to explore whether children with special education exceptionality reliably qualify for a clinical ASD diagnosis. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Differences between IDEA requirements and DSM-5 diagnostic criteria (i.e., focusing on different aspects of ASD). * Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): References include CDC ADDM prevalence reports (2018, 2020, 2022) and papers on surveillance collaboration and education vs clinical diagnoses.
key_numbers: Overall n=873; record source subgroups: Single Source n=529, Multiple Source n=344
8-year-old children in Arizona ADDM surveillance cycles
Recognition categories: 48% education-only, 18% ICD-only, 29% both, 6% neither (n=873).
Multiple sources: OR=1.77 for exceptionality (Holm p=0.002).
Multiple sources: OR=12.57 for ICD code (Holm p<0.001).
takeaways: Recognition categories show limited overlap between educational and clinical systems (n=873): 48% education-only, 18% ICD-only, 29% both, 6% neither.
Among children with multiple sources, agreement between exceptionality and ICD code is near zero: Cohen’s κ \= -0.001

Multinomial regression (statistically significant predictors shown in Figure 1) reports relative risk ratios by category for: race/ethnicity, intellectual disability status (unknown), surveillance year, SVI status, and multiple sources.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What exactly counts as a “record source” (education file, clinician record, both, other), and how are “single” vs “multiple” sources defined?

what_to_ask.question: The overlap plot shows 48% “exceptionality-only” and 18% “ICD-only.” What do you think is the biggest driver of this asymmetry?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: What was the reference standard for sensitivity/specificity/PPV/NPV? How should we interpret specificity being 11% (all children) and 17% (multiple sources)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: If a child is “education-only,” what does that typically mean for access to clinical services in Arizona (based on your experience with these records)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: You note “full ADDM Network confirmation.” What analysis plan would you use—pooled model, site-specific models, or meta-analysis

collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.
